data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was driven by a systemic fragility in the palatial redistribution economy where centralized storage and specialized labor created a single point of failure; when external shocks (drought, migration) disrupted input flows, the rigid bureaucratic apparatus could not adapt, leading to cascading institutional dissolution.", "mechanism": "Positive feedback loop of centralization reducing resilience: as states specialized to maximize surplus extraction, they lost the modular redundancy required to absorb local failures, causing a domino effect across the interconnected trade network.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Cyprus minor settlements"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence of flexible, decentralized survival strategies within palace centers during the initial shock phase", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Aligns with the mechanist school (ba68...) and echoes the systemic failure hypothesis of 919f... but emphasizes the structural rigidity rather than just the trigger event."}
